input_row.title: Ensure the Key Vault is Recoverable
input_row.description: Key Vaults contain object keys, secrets, and certificates. Deletion of a Key Vault can cause immediate data loss or loss of security functions (authentication, validation, verification, non-repudiation, etc.) supported by the Key Vault objects. It is recommended the Key Vault be made recoverable by enabling the "Do Not Purge" and "Soft Delete" functions. This is in order to prevent loss of encrypted data, including storage accounts, SQL databases, and/or dependent services provided by Key Vault objects (Keys, Secrets, Certificates) etc. This may happen in the case of accidental deletion by a user or from disruptive activity by a malicious user. NOTE: In February 2025, Microsoft will enable soft-delete protection on all key vaults, and users will no longer be able to opt out of or turn off soft-delete. WARNING: A current limitation is that role assignments disappearing when Key Vault is deleted. All role assignments will need to be recreated after recovery.
input_row.rationale: Users may accidentally run delete/purge commands on a Key Vault, or an attacker or malicious user may do so deliberately in order to cause disruption. Deleting or purging a Key Vault leads to immediate data loss, as keys encrypting data and secrets/certificates allowing access/services will become non-accessible. Setting enablePurgeProtection to "true" for a Key Vault ensures that even if Key Vault is deleted, Key Vault itself or its objects remain recoverable for the next 90 days. Key Vault/objects can either be recovered or purged (permanent deletion) during those 90 days. If no action is taken, the key vault and its objects will subsequently be purged. Enabling the enablePurgeProtection parameter on Key Vaults ensures that Key Vaults and their objects cannot be deleted/purged permanently. Impact: Once purge-protection and soft-delete are enabled for a Key Vault, the action is irreversible.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to Key Vaults. 2. Click the name of a Key Vault. 3. Under Settings, click Properties. 4. Next to Purge protection, ensure that Enable purge protection (enforce a mandatory retention period for deleted vaults and vault objects) is selected. 5. Repeat steps 1-4 for each Key Vault. Audit from Azure CLI List all Key Vaults: az resource list --query "[?type=='Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults']" For each Key Vault, ensure enablePurgeProtection is set to true: az resource show --id /subscriptions/xxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx- xxxxxxxxxxxx/resourceGroups/<resourceGroupName>/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault /vaults/<keyVaultName> Audit from PowerShell List all Key Vaults: Get-AzKeyVault For each Key Vault run the following command: Get-AzKeyVault -VaultName <Vault Name> Ensure EnablePurgeProtection is set to True. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 0b60c0b2-2dc2-4e1c-b5c9-abbed971de53 - Name: 'Key vaults should have deletion protection enabled'
input_row.remediation: To enable "Do Not Purge" and "Soft Delete" for a Key Vault: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to Key Vaults. 2. Click the name of a Key Vault. 3. Under Settings, click Properties. 4. Select the radio button next to Enable purge protection (enforce a mandatory retention period for deleted vaults and vault objects). Note: Once enabled, this option cannot be disabled. 5. Click Save. 6. Repeat steps 1-5 for each Key Vault requiring remediation.  Remediate from Azure CLI az resource update --id /subscriptions/xxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx- xxxxxxxxxxxx/resourceGroups/<resourceGroupName>/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault /vaults/<keyVaultName> --set properties.enablePurgeProtection=true Remediate from PowerShell Update-AzKeyVault -VaultName <vaultName -ResourceGroupName <resourceGroupName -EnablePurgeProtection Default Value: When a new Key Vault is created, • enableSoftDelete is enabled by default, and • enablePurgeProtection is disabled by default. NOTE: In February 2025, Microsoft will enable soft-delete protection on all key vaults, and users will no longer be able to opt out of or turn off soft-delete. References: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/key-vault-soft-delete-cli 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/soft-delete-overview 3. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-governance- strategy#gs-8-define-and-implement-backup-and-recovery-strategy 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-data- protection#dp-8-ensure-security-of-key-and-certificate-repository Additional Information: When a key is used for SQL server TDE or Encrypting Storage Account, both the features "Do Not Purge" and "Soft Delete" are enabled for the corresponding Key Vault by default by Azure Backend. WARNING: A current limitation of the soft-delete feature across all Azure services is role assignments disappearing when Key Vault is deleted. All role assignments will need to be recreated after recovery.
input_row.impact: Once purge-protection and soft-delete are enabled for a Key Vault, the action is irreversible.
input_row.reference: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/key-vault-soft-delete-cli 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/soft-delete-overview 3. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-governance- strategy#gs-8-define-and-implement-backup-and-recovery-strategy 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-data- protection#dp-8-ensure-security-of-key-and-certificate-repository Additional Information: When a key is used for SQL server TDE or Encrypting Storage Account, both the features "Do Not Purge" and "Soft Delete" are enabled for the corresponding Key Vault by default by Azure Backend. WARNING: A current limitation of the soft-delete feature across all Azure services is role assignments disappearing when Key Vault is deleted. All role assignments will need to be recreated after recovery.

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit process for ensuring the Key Vault is recoverable can be conducted using Azure CLI and PowerShell commands as mentioned in the provided details. Specific Azure APIs allow checking the properties of Key Vaults, such as `enablePurgeProtection`, which is explicitly queried through commands and APIs.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_keyvault_recovery_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
The automated program will perform the following checks:

1. **Enumerate Key Vaults:**
   - Use Azure CLI command to list all Key Vaults:
     ```
     az resource list --query "[?type=='Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults']"
     ```
   - Alternatively, use PowerShell:
     ```
     Get-AzKeyVault
     ```

2. **Check `enablePurgeProtection` for Each Key Vault:**
   - For each Key Vault identified, verify whether the `enablePurgeProtection` property is set to `true` using the following Azure CLI command:
     ```
     az resource show --id /subscriptions/{subscription_id}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/{keyVaultName} --query "properties.enablePurgeProtection"
     ```
   - In PowerShell, run:
     ```
     $keyVault = Get-AzKeyVault -VaultName <Vault Name>
     $keyVault.EnablePurgeProtection
     ```

3. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** The `enablePurgeProtection` property must be `true`.
   - **Fail:** The `enablePurgeProtection` property is `false` or does not exist.

4. **APIs:**
   - The program utilizes Azure Resource Manager APIs for both querying and auditing Key Vault properties.

This structured automated approach allows for efficient checking of compliance against the CIS Benchmark related to the recoverability of Key Vaults.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The use of Azure CLI and PowerShell commands is appropriate for automating the process of checking Key Vault properties. Azure APIs, CLI, and PowerShell cmdlets are suitable for this task, as they provide direct access to the necessary properties of Key Vaults, such as `enablePurgeProtection`.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The Azure CLI and PowerShell commands provided are valid for enumerating Key Vaults and checking the `enablePurgeProtection` property. However, the automation details could be improved by including checks for the `enableSoftDelete` property, as both `Do Not Purge` and `Soft Delete` are recommended for ensuring recoverability.
- The program name follows the convention: azure_keyvault_recovery_enabled, which is clear and descriptive.
- The manual steps are not applicable in this context as the approach is automated.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Include `enableSoftDelete` Check:** The audit should also verify that the `enableSoftDelete` property is set to `true`, as both properties are crucial for ensuring the Key Vault is recoverable.
- **Error Handling and Logging:** Enhance the automation script to include error handling and logging to capture any issues during execution, such as permissions errors or API failures.
- **Documentation and Comments:** Add comments and documentation within the script to explain each step, making it easier for future auditors to understand and maintain the script.
- **Subscription and Resource Group Iteration:** Consider iterating over multiple subscriptions and resource groups if applicable, to ensure comprehensive coverage.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- I have high confidence in this assessment due to the use of well-documented Azure CLI and PowerShell commands and the structured approach to automation.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- Modify the automation script to include checks for the `enableSoftDelete` property in addition to `enablePurgeProtection`. This will ensure full compliance with the CIS Benchmark recommendation for Key Vault recoverability.
- Ensure that the script includes robust error handling and logging mechanisms to improve reliability and traceability.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the audit process for ensuring Key Vault recoverability can be automated using Azure CLI and PowerShell commands. The necessary properties, such as `enablePurgeProtection`, are accessible through Azure APIs, making automation feasible and efficient. This approach leverages existing Azure capabilities to systematically verify compliance across all Key Vaults.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_keyvault_recovery_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **Azure Resource/Service:** Key Vaults
- **API/CLI Method:** `az resource list`, `az resource show`
- **Properties to Check:** `enablePurgeProtection`, `enableSoftDelete`
- **Pass Criteria:** Both `enablePurgeProtection` and `enableSoftDelete` are set to `true`
- **Fail Criteria:** Either `enablePurgeProtection` or `enableSoftDelete` is not set to `true`

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There were no significant disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported an automated approach. The second opinion suggested including checks for `enableSoftDelete`, which aligns with the compliance control's requirements and has been incorporated into the final decision.
